q: If A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} and B = {3, 4, 5, 6, 7}, then A △ B (symmetric difference) is:
exp: Symmetric difference A △ B = (A ∪ B) − (A ∩ B). A ∩ B = {3,4,5}, A ∪ B = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7}. So A △ B = {1,2,6,7}. Elements in A but not B plus elements in B but not A.

q: The number of subsets of a set with n elements is:
exp: A set with n elements has exactly 2^n subsets, including the empty set and the set itself. For example, {a,b} has 4 subsets: {}, {a}, {b}, {a,b} = 2^2 = 4.

q: If n(A) = 20, n(B) = 30, and n(A ∪ B) = 40, then n(A ∩ B) is:
exp: By inclusion-exclusion principle: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B). So 40 = 20 + 30 − n(A ∩ B), giving n(A ∩ B) = 10.

q: Which of the following is NOT a real number?
exp: √(−4) = 2i, which is an imaginary number, not a real number. √4 = 2, −√5, and π are all real numbers. The square root of a negative number is always imaginary.

q: The interval (−3, 5] in set notation is:
exp: The notation (−3, 5] means open at −3 (not included, shown by parenthesis) and closed at 5 (included, shown by bracket). So the correct set-builder form is −3 < x ≤ 5.

q: |2x − 5| < 3 implies:
exp: |2x − 5| < 3 gives −3 < 2x − 5 < 3. Adding 5: 2 < 2x < 8. Dividing by 2: 1 < x < 4. The absolute value inequality |A| < k gives −k < A < k.

q: The solution of |x − 2| = 5 is:
exp: |x − 2| = 5 means x − 2 = 5 or x − 2 = −5. Solving: x = 7 or x = −3. Both values satisfy the original equation. Always check both cases for absolute value equations.

q: De Morgan's law states that (A ∩ B)' equals:
exp: De Morgan's law: (A ∩ B)' = A' ∪ B' and (A ∪ B)' = A' ∩ B'. The complement of an intersection is the union of complements. This is a fundamental law in set theory and logic.

q: The negation of 'All students are intelligent' is:
opts: A) Some students are not intelligent
C) All students are not intelligent
exp: The negation of 'All P are Q' is 'Some P are not Q' (at least one). The negation of a universal statement is an existential statement. 'No student is intelligent' is the negation of 'Some students are intelligent', which is different.

q: The contrapositive of 'If p then q' (p → q) is:
exp: The contrapositive of p → q is ¬q → ¬p. Importantly, a statement and its contrapositive are logically equivalent (always have the same truth value). The inverse is ¬p → ¬q and the converse is q → p.

q: Which of the following is a tautology?
exp: p ∨ ¬p is always TRUE regardless of the truth value of p (Law of Excluded Middle), making it a tautology. p ∧ ¬p is always FALSE (contradiction). p → q and p ∨ q depend on values of p and q.

q: If A ⊂ B, then A ∩ B equals:
exp: If A ⊂ B (A is a subset of B), every element of A is also in B. So A ∩ B = A (the common elements are all of A). Also, A ∪ B = B when A ⊂ B.

q: The number of elements in the power set of {a, b, c, d} is:
exp: Power set P(A) of a set A with n elements has 2^n elements. For {a,b,c,d}, n = 4, so |P(A)| = 2^4 = 16. This includes ∅ and the set {a,b,c,d} itself.

q: If p is TRUE and q is FALSE, the truth value of p ↔ q is:
opts: D) TRUE only if p ≠ q
exp: p ↔ q (biconditional) is TRUE when both have the same truth value, and FALSE when they differ. Here p = T and q = F (different), so p ↔ q = FALSE. The biconditional means 'p if and only if q'.

q: Which real number property states a + 0 = a?
exp: a + 0 = a is the additive identity property, where 0 is the identity element for addition. The additive inverse is a + (−a) = 0. The multiplicative identity is a × 1 = a.

q: The set of irrational numbers is:
exp: Irrational numbers are real numbers that are NOT rational. So irrational numbers = ℝ \ ℚ (real numbers minus rational numbers). Examples: √2, π, e. They cannot be expressed as p/q where p,q ∈ ℤ, q ≠ 0.

q: The interval [2, ∞) means:
opts: A) All real numbers greater than or equal to 2
B) All real numbers greater than 2
D) All real numbers from 2 to infinity including infinity
exp: [2, ∞) = {x ∈ ℝ : x ≥ 2}. The bracket at 2 means 2 is included. The parenthesis at ∞ is always used since infinity is not a real number and cannot be 'included'. Option D is wrong because infinity is never included.

q: If n(A) = 15, n(B) = 20, n(A ∩ B) = 8, then n(A ∪ B) is:
exp: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B) = 15 + 20 − 8 = 27. This is the inclusion-exclusion formula. We subtract the intersection to avoid double-counting elements that belong to both sets.

q: The truth value of the statement 'If 2 + 2 = 5, then the moon is made of cheese' is:
exp: An implication p → q is FALSE only when p is TRUE and q is FALSE. Here p = '2+2=5' is FALSE. When the hypothesis is false, the implication is always TRUE (vacuously true), regardless of the conclusion. This is a common logic trap.

q: |x| > 3 implies:
exp: |x| > 3 means the distance of x from 0 is greater than 3. This gives x > 3 OR x < −3. For absolute value: |A| > k (k > 0) gives A > k or A < −k. Contrast with |A| < k which gives −k < A < k.

q: Which of the following is equivalent to p → q?
exp: p → q ≡ ¬p ∨ q. This can be verified by truth table. The implication is false only when p is true and q is false, which is exactly when ¬p ∨ q is false. This equivalence is frequently tested in entrance exams.

q: If A = {x : x is a prime number, x < 10}, the number of elements in A is:
exp: Primes less than 10: 2, 3, 5, 7. So A = {2, 3, 5, 7} and n(A) = 4. Note: 1 is NOT prime by definition. 2 is the only even prime. Students often include 1 or forget that 2 is prime.

q: The converse of 'If it rains, the ground is wet' is:
opts: A) If the ground is wet, it rains
B) If it does not rain, the ground is not wet
C) If the ground is not wet, it does not rain
D) It rains and the ground is wet
exp: Converse of p → q is q → p. Original: Rain → Ground wet. Converse: Ground wet → Rain. Note that the converse is NOT equivalent to the original (the ground could be wet for other reasons). The contrapositive (option C) is equivalent to the original.

q: The absolute value of −|−7| is:
exp: Step by step: |−7| = 7. Then −|−7| = −7. Then |−7| = 7? No, the question asks for −|−7|. So: first |−7| = 7, then −(7) = −7. The outer absolute value is NOT applied here. The expression is −|−7| = −7. This is a common sign trap.

q: Which law states A ∪ (B ∩ C) = (A ∪ B) ∩ (A ∪ C)?
exp: The Distributive law states that union distributes over intersection: A ∪ (B ∩ C) = (A ∪ B) ∩ (A ∪ C). Similarly, A ∩ (B ∪ C) = (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). This mirrors the distributive property of multiplication over addition in algebra.

q: If (A ∪ B)' = ∅, then:
exp: (A ∪ B)' = ∅ means the complement of A ∪ B is empty, so nothing is outside A ∪ B. This means A ∪ B contains all elements, i.e., A ∪ B = U. The complement of U is ∅, confirming this.

q: The logical expression ¬(p ∧ q) is equivalent to:
exp: By De Morgan's law for logic: ¬(p ∧ q) = ¬p ∨ ¬q. Similarly ¬(p ∨ q) = ¬p ∧ ¬q. This is the logical analogue of De Morgan's set law. It is among the most frequently tested logic identities.

q: Which of the following sets is a null set?
exp: x² = −1 has no real solutions, so option A is null. x² = 1 gives x = ±1, not null. Even primes greater than 2: there are none (2 is the only even prime), so option C is also null. Hence both A and C are null sets, making D correct.

q: Which of the following is NOT a rational number?
exp: √2 is irrational — it cannot be expressed as p/q. 22/7 is rational (it's p/q form). 0.333... = 1/3, a rational number. −5 = −5/1 is rational. Note: 22/7 ≠ π; π is irrational but 22/7 is rational.

q: The statement p ∧ (p → q) → q is a:
exp: This is the modus ponens tautology. If p is true and p → q is true, then q must be true. Verify by truth table: when p=T, q=T: T∧T→T = T; p=T, q=F: T∧F→F = T∧T = T... checking all cases gives always TRUE. So it's a tautology.

A' (complement of A) = U − A = all elements in universal set that are NOT in A. U = {1,...,10}, A = {2,4,6,8,10} (even numbers). So A' = {1,3,5,7,9} (odd numbers). The complement depends on the universal set.

q: The solution set of |3x − 6| = 0 is:
exp: |3x − 6| = 0 implies 3x − 6 = 0 (absolute value is zero only when the expression itself is zero). So 3x = 6, x = 2. The solution set is {2}. This is different from |3x − 6| = 0 having two solutions; |A| = 0 has only one solution A = 0.

q: In a survey, 60% students like mathematics, 50% like science, and 30% like both. The percentage who like neither is:
exp: n(M ∪ S) = n(M) + n(S) − n(M ∩ S) = 60 + 50 − 30 = 80%. So 80% like at least one subject. Those who like neither = 100 − 80 = 20%. This is a classic three-set problem type often seen in CEE/IOE.

q: The negation of 'p ∧ q' is logically equivalent to:
exp: By De Morgan's Law for logic: ¬(p ∧ q) ≡ ¬p ∨ ¬q. This is one of the two De Morgan's laws. Remember: to negate a conjunction, take disjunction of negations; to negate a disjunction, take conjunction of negations.

q: Which of the following represents an open interval?
exp: (a, b) is a fully open interval where neither endpoint is included, i.e., a < x < b. [a, b] is closed (both included). [a, b) is half-open (closed at a, open at b). (a, b] is half-open (open at a, closed at b).

q: If A ∩ B = ∅, A and B are called:
exp: When A ∩ B = ∅ (no common elements), A and B are called disjoint sets. Equal sets have exactly the same elements. Equivalent sets have the same number of elements. Overlapping sets have at least one common element.

q: The value of |−5| + |3 − 8| is:
exp: |−5| = 5. |3 − 8| = |−5| = 5. So the sum = 5 + 5 = 10. Absolute value always gives non-negative result. Students often make sign errors when computing absolute values of negative expressions.

q: The inverse of the statement p → q is:
exp: The inverse of p → q is ¬p → ¬q (negate both parts without switching). The converse is q → p (switch). The contrapositive is ¬q → ¬p (switch and negate). Only the contrapositive is logically equivalent to the original.

q: Which of the following is a well-defined set?
opts: A) {x : x is a good student}
B) {x : x is a prime number < 20}
C) {x : x is a beautiful girl}
exp: A set is well-defined if we can clearly determine whether any object belongs to it or not. 'Prime number < 20' is well-defined: {2,3,5,7,11,13,17,19}. 'Good', 'beautiful', 'tall' are subjective and hence not well-defined.

q: For any set A, A ∪ A' equals:
exp: A ∪ A' = U (universal set). Every element is either in A or not in A (its complement). Together they cover everything. This is the law of excluded middle in set theory. Also, A ∩ A' = ∅ (law of contradiction).

q: The compound statement 'p or q' (p ∨ q) is FALSE when:
opts: A) Both p and q are false
B) p is true and q is false
C) p is false and q is true
D) Both p and q are true
exp: p ∨ q is FALSE only when BOTH p and q are FALSE. In all other cases (at least one true), p ∨ q is TRUE. This is inclusive OR. The disjunction requires at least one true component to be true.

q: If |2x + 3| ≤ 7, then the solution is:
exp: |2x + 3| ≤ 7 gives −7 ≤ 2x + 3 ≤ 7. Subtract 3: −10 ≤ 2x ≤ 4. Divide by 2: −5 ≤ x ≤ 2. The solution is a closed interval [−5, 2]. For ≤ type, the result is always a closed bounded interval.
